description: Systematic code quality improvements across the platform: better variable naming, clearer file names, missing JSDoc documentation, type safety improvements, magic number extraction, and consistent error handling patterns.

userStories.title: Improve variable naming in validate-icon.ts
userStories.description: As a developer, I want descriptive variable names in the icon validation module so that the color-conversion logic is readable without domain knowledge.
acceptanceCriteria: All single-letter variables (r, g, b, h, s, l, a, c, x, m, v, w) in validate-icon.ts are replaced with descriptive names
Color components use names like redValue, greenValue, blueValue, hueValue, saturation, lightness, alphaChannel
HSL conversion variables use names like chroma, xComponent, matchValue
Trimmed-value variables use names like trimmedValue, normalizedValue instead of v
Width/height variables use widthValue, heightValue instead of w, h
All existing tests still pass (no logic changes, only renames)
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: File: platform/plugin-tools/src/validate-icon.ts (439 lines). This file has the most egregious naming. Key areas: line 130 `const h = hex.trim()`, lines 135-137 and 142-144 for RGB parsing, lines 155-157 for HSL conversion (c/x/m), line 180 `const h = Math.max(...)`, lines 189/225/347 `const v = ...`, lines 268-269 `const w/h`, lines 369-382 RGBA/RGB channels. Only rename variables — do not change any logic.

For utils.ts: grep for imports of './utils' or '../lib/utils' in side-panel/. For bg-log-state.ts: grep for imports of './bg-log-state' in background.ts and other files. Use LSP rename or manual find-replace for all import paths. Verify no runtime references break (e.g., dynamic imports).

userStories.title: Extract magic numbers into named constants in browser-extension
userStories.description: As a developer, I want all hardcoded timeout and delay values to be named constants so that their purpose is clear and they can be tuned from one location.
acceptanceCriteria: A constants section or file (e.g., extending platform/browser-extension/src/constants.ts) contains named constants for all timeout/delay values used in the side-panel components and browser-commands
3000ms timeout in PluginCard.tsx (line ~61) uses a named constant (e.g., ERROR_DISPLAY_DURATION_MS)
3000ms timeout in ReturningUserEmptyState.tsx (line ~25) uses a named constant (e.g., CONFIRMATION_DISPLAY_DURATION_MS)
2000ms timeout in OutdatedPluginsBadge.tsx (line ~21) uses a named constant (e.g., COPIED_INDICATOR_DURATION_MS)
200ms interval in ConfirmationDialog.tsx (line ~32) uses a named constant (e.g., CONFIRMATION_POLL_INTERVAL_MS)
200ms delay in iife-injection.ts (line ~233) uses a named constant (e.g., INJECTION_RETRY_DELAY_MS)
100ms and 50ms delays in browser-commands.ts (lines ~292, ~2111) use named constants
All existing tests still pass
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: Check platform/browser-extension/src/constants.ts for existing constants to extend. Side-panel component constants can live in a side-panel-specific constants file if preferred (e.g., platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/constants.ts). The key principle: every numeric literal used as a timeout or delay should have a descriptive name. Import the constants where used. Do NOT change the actual values — only extract them into named constants.

userStories.title: Reduce as-any usage in plugin-tools build.ts IIFE wrapper
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the IIFE wrapper generation code to use typed helpers for globalThis access so that the 12 'as any' casts are minimized.
acceptanceCriteria: The number of 'as any' casts in platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts is reduced
globalThis.__openTabs access is handled through a typed pattern (e.g., a declared global interface or typed accessor in the generated IIFE code)
The 'plugin.onDeactivate = undefined as any' pattern (lines ~636, ~651) is replaced with a safer alternative
The 'origHandle.apply(this, handleArgs as any)' pattern (line ~573) is replaced with proper typing if possible
Generated IIFE code still functions correctly — the adapter registration, lifecycle hooks, and log transport work as before
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: File: platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts. The 'as any' casts are in the IIFE wrapper template string (lines 495-651). This is generated JavaScript code that runs in the browser page context, so TypeScript types don't apply at runtime. The challenge is that the template string is embedded in TypeScript, so tsc checks the string interpolation types. Consider: (1) declaring a globalThis augmentation interface for __openTabs, (2) using a typed helper function for property access, or (3) extracting the IIFE template to a separate .js template file that tsc doesn't check. The 'undefined as any' for onDeactivate could use 'delete' operator or optional chaining instead. Be careful not to break the runtime behavior of the generated IIFE.

userStories.title: Add JSDoc to browser command handler functions

acceptanceCriteria: At minimum, the following high-use handlers have JSDoc: handleBrowserListTabs, handleBrowserOpenTab, handleBrowserCloseTab, handleBrowserNavigateTab, handleBrowserFocusTab, handleBrowserGetTabInfo, handleBrowserGetPageHtml, handleBrowserGetStorage, handleBrowserTypeText, handleBrowserGetCookies, handleBrowserSetCookie, handleBrowserExecuteScript, handleBrowserScreenshotTab, handleBrowserClickElement, handleBrowserQueryElements
Each JSDoc describes: (1) what the handler does, (2) what params it expects, (3) what it returns
JSDoc descriptions are concise — one or two sentences maximum

bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: File: platform/browser-extension/src/browser-commands.ts (2121 lines). There are 40+ exported handler functions. Focus on the top 15 most important ones listed in the acceptance criteria. Read each function to write an accurate description — do not guess. The params are typed objects so just describe what the handler does and what it returns. Keep it terse: /** Lists all open Chrome tabs with their IDs, URLs, and titles. */ style.
